topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a lead weight, instantly sinking my mood. My stomach tightened into a hard knot. I clicked the attachment, and the headline swam into view: "Groundbreaking Analysis by Eleanor Vance." My fingers trembled slightly as I scanned the byline, the name that belonged to a respected, established journalist, not me. A slow, burning heat rose up my neck. I reread the article, the words I had poured my soul into, now belonging to a stranger. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. A bitter laugh escaped my lips, dry and sharp. I felt a profound sense of being utterly bypassed, a hollow echo where my contribution should have been. I began to type a reply, my movements stiff and deliberate, every keystroke laced with cold, controlled fury.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my breath catching in my throat. The realization hit me with a sickening lurch, stealing the air from my lungs. My hands clenched into fists so tight my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. I could feel the flush creeping up my cheeks, a hot tide of indignation washing over me. It was impossible. The data, the insights, the sheer effort I had invested—it was all there, meticulously laid out, yet it was all attributed to someone else. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal against the floor sounding deafening in the quiet office. I paced three steps toward the window, my jaw tight. The view of the city below seemed mocking in its indifference. When I finally turned back to the keyboard, my voice, when I finally typed, was low and dangerously steady.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I didn't move immediately. I just sat there, frozen, absorbing the sheer audacity of it. My gaze dropped to the name printed beneath the title, and a deep, unpleasant churning started in my gut. I slowly reached out and touched the screen, my fingertips pressing against the offending text as if to confirm its reality. A tight, almost painful tension coiled in my shoulders. I let out a slow, deliberate exhale, the sound ragged. I stood up, walking to the filing cabinet, running my hand over the cold metal. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready to spring. I pulled out my phone, my expression set in a mask of chilling composure, preparing to dismantle this deception piece by agonizing piece.
